stem:
A 45-year-old man presents at a community pharmacy on 30 October 2025 with an NHS prescription for morphine sulfate 10mg/5mL oral solution. The prescription is dated 1 October 2025.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Contact the prescriber to request they change the date to 30 October 2025.

options.label: B
options.text: Dispense the prescription as it is within the 28-day validity period.

options.label: C
options.text: Dispense the prescription but inform the man he must collect any remaining balance within 24 hours.

options.label: D
options.text: Inform the man that the prescription has expired and refer him back to his prescriber.

options.label: E
options.text: Make a record in the Controlled Drugs register and dispense the medication.

explain_correct: Morphine sulfate is a Schedule 2 CD. Schedule 2, 3 and 4 CD prescriptions are valid for 28 days from the appropriate date. Per RPS/Home Office guidance, this means the prescription is valid up to and including (appropriate date + 28). For a prescription dated 1 October, the last valid day is 29 October. Presentation on 30 October is past validity, so the pharmacist cannot lawfully dispense and must refer the patient back to the prescriber for a new prescription.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; Reg 15 MDR 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: Illegal; Regulation 15 of MDR 2001 allows pharmacists to correct minor spelling/omissions but NOT the date.
explain_wrong.B: Schedule 2 CDs are valid for 28 days; the 28th day was 29 October.
explain_wrong.C: the legal validity for dispensing has already passed.
explain_wrong.E: dispensing an expired CD is a breach of the MDR 2001.

stem:
A 32-year-old woman requests an emergency supply of phenobarbital 60mg tablets at 19:00 on a Saturday for epilepsy.

What is the maximum quantity of phenobarbital that can be supplied in this scenario?
explain_correct: Maximum quantity for phenobarbital ES for epilepsy is 5 days. HMR 2012 Schedule 18 allows phenobarbital for epilepsy as the only exception for CD ES at patient request, limited to 5 days.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: while 3 days is a possible supply, it is not the legal maximum.
explain_wrong.C: standard 28-day ES rules do not apply to Schedule 3 CDs.
explain_wrong.D: 30 days is for standard POMs, not phenobarbital.
explain_wrong.E: phenobarbital is explicitly exempted from the CD ES ban specifically for epilepsy.

stem:
A GP requests stock of diamorphine 10mg ampoules using practice letterhead.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: B
options.text: Inform GP that a mandatory FP10CDF form is required

options.label: D
options.text: Supply if GP provides signed prescription in 72 hours

explain_correct: Schedule 2 and 3 CDs require a mandatory requisition form (FP10CDF). MDR 2001 Reg 14 requires a mandatory approved form for all Sch 2 and 3 stock requisitions.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: pharmacists can legally fulfill requisitions if documentation is correct.
explain_wrong.C: GPs can hold stock of Sch 2 CDs for professional use.
explain_wrong.D: requisitions must be provided at the time of supply.
explain_wrong.E: supplying without the FP10CDF form is a criminal offence.

stem:
Reviewing Schedule 3 CDs for safe custody storage.

Which of the following Schedule 3 Controlled Drugs is legally required to be stored in the Controlled Drug cabinet?
explain_correct: Temazepam and buprenorphine are not exempt from the Safe Custody Regulations 1973. temazepam and buprenorphine are Sch 3 CDs that MUST be in the cabinet.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: gabapentin is a Schedule 3 CD but is exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.B: midazolam is exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.C: phenobarbital is exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.E: tramadol is exempt from safe custody.

stem:
Making an entry for oxycodone capsules (Schedule 2).

Which piece of information is a legal requirement to record in the Controlled Drugs register?

options.label: E
options.text: Whether proof of identity was requested

explain_correct: Proof of identity records are mandatory for Sch 2 CD supplies. MDR 2001 requires recording if ID was requested and provided for Schedule 2 supplies to patients/representatives.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; Reg 19 MDR 2001 (record keeping); MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: batch/expiry is not a legal requirement for the CD register.
explain_wrong.B: checking pharmacist name is good practice but not legally mandated in the register columns.
explain_wrong.C: indication is never a register requirement.
explain_wrong.D: address is required, but DOB is not.

stem:
Destroying expired pethidine tablets (Schedule 2).

Which of the following individuals is an appropriate person to authorize a witness for the destruction?
explain_correct: Stock CD destruction requires an authorized witness appointed by the CDAO. CDAOs are responsible for appointing and authorizing witnesses for CD destruction under the Health Act 2006.
citation: Reg 27 MDR 2001 (destruction); MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.B: GPs have no authority over pharmacy CD stock destruction.
explain_wrong.C: the owner cannot authorize their own witness.
explain_wrong.D: while a police officer can witness, they are not the authorizing body.
explain_wrong.E: while they manage SOPs, the CDAO holds the legal authority.

stem:
A woman of childbearing potential is prescribed valproate but uses no contraception.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Advise patient to stop taking if pregnant

options.label: B
options.text: Confirm Annual Risk Acknowledgement Form and PPP enrollment with prescriber

explain_correct: Pharmacists must ensure the PPP is in place for women of childbearing potential on valproate. pharmacists must verify that the Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) requirements are met.
citation: MHRA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: patients should never stop valproate abruptly; they must seek medical review.
explain_wrong.C: providing a card is insufficient if the patient is not on highly effective contraception.
explain_wrong.D: while 30-day supply is recommended, it does not solve the safety compliance issue.
explain_wrong.E: pregnancy tests are conducted by the specialist/GP, not the pharmacist as a condition for supply.

stem: How long must the Controlled Drugs register be retained from the date of the last entry?
explain_correct: Retention period for CD registers is 2 years from the date of the last entry. MDR 2001 requires 2-year retention for CD registers.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; Reg 19 MDR 2001 (record keeping); MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: too short.
explain_wrong.C: 5 years is for RP records in most cases.
explain_wrong.D: 6 years is for financial records, not CDs.
explain_wrong.E: excessive.

stem:
A GP requests an emergency supply of methylphenidate (Schedule 2) over the phone.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Inform GP that ES cannot be made for Schedule 2 CDs

options.label: D
options.text: Supply if GP emails electronic signature

options.label: E
options.text: Supply if prescription provided in 72 hours

explain_correct: Prescriber-requested emergency supplies are prohibited for Schedule 2 and 3 CDs. HMR 2012 strictly prohibits emergency supply of Schedule 2 and 3 CDs at the request of a prescriber.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; HMR 2012 Reg 224/225 (emergency supply); MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.B: this is an illegal supply.
explain_wrong.C: Sch 2 CDs cannot be supplied in an emergency via phone.
explain_wrong.D: electronic signature does not bypass the CD ES prohibition.
explain_wrong.E: the 72-hour rule applies only to standard POMs, not Sch 2/3 CDs.

stem:
A man traveling to the US for 4 months with methadone (Schedule 2).

Which is the most appropriate advice for the pharmacist to provide?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply for personal import/export licence from Home Office

options.label: B
options.text: Carry doctor's letter (no licence needed for 4 months)

options.label: C
options.text: Pharmacy can post the remaining supply

explain_correct: Home Office licenses are mandatory for travel exceeding 3 months with Schedule 2-4 CDs. a Home Office licence is legally required for carrying > 3 months' supply of CDs.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.B: while a letter is needed, it does not replace a licence for a 4-month duration.
explain_wrong.C: posting CDs internationally is generally prohibited.
explain_wrong.D: advice must address the full travel duration.
explain_wrong.E: travel with Sch 2 CDs without documentation risks seizure.

stem:
Temazepam prescription omits formulation 'tablets'.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: C
options.text: Make a note in CD register

explain_correct: Omissions of formulation on Schedule 2 or 3 CD prescriptions must be corrected by the prescriber. the pharmacist cannot legally amend an omitted formulation on a CD prescription.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; Reg 15 MDR 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: Reg 15 correction powers generally exclude 'form' omissions.
explain_wrong.B: dispensing an incomplete CD prescription is illegal.
explain_wrong.C: register entries cannot fix invalid prescriptions.
explain_wrong.D: a letter does not make the prescription legal.

stem: Which of the following Controlled Drugs is classified as Schedule 2?
explain_correct: Lisdexamfetamine is classified under Schedule 2 of the MDR 2001. lisdexamfetamine is a stimulant in Schedule 2.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: buprenorphine is Schedule 3.
explain_wrong.B: gabapentin is Schedule 3.
explain_wrong.D: midazolam is Schedule 3.
explain_wrong.E: tramadol is Schedule 3.

stem: What is the Home Office recommendation for recording the supply of Sativex?

options.label: A
options.text: Home Office strongly recommends CD register use

explain_correct: Sativex requires Home Office recommended CD register entries despite its Schedule 4 status. although Sativex is Schedule 4 Part I, the Home Office strongly recommends record-keeping in the CD register.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001; Reg 19 MDR 2001 (record keeping); HMR 2012 Reg 254 (POM register); MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.B: as a POM, some record is always required.
explain_wrong.C: it is a human medicine.
explain_wrong.D: standard POM retention is 2 years for entries.
explain_wrong.E: witnessing is not legally mandated for Sch 4 CDs.

stem:
Suspicious private prescription for oxycodone on letterhead (not FP10PCD).

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: B
options.text: Contact prescriber via verified source number

options.label: D
options.text: Keep and ask patient to return later

explain_correct: Verification of suspicious CD prescriptions via a trusted source is mandatory practice. suspicious CD prescriptions must be verified using a number from a separate, trusted source.
citation: MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: the first step is clinical and legal verification.
explain_wrong.C: oxycodone must be on a mandatory FP10PCD form if private.
explain_wrong.D: keeping a prescription without verification is insufficient.
explain_wrong.E: GPs can write oxycodone but must use the correct form.

stem: Select the legal requirement from the list that ensures the recipient's identity is linked to the authority to possess.
explain_correct: The signature on a CD requisition is the legal instrument that links the requisition to the authority of the recipient. Other fields identify or describe, but only the signature provides personal accountability and authorisation.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001, Regulation 14 (requisitions). MEP Edition 45, section 3.6.5.

explain_wrong.A: Address identifies the location but does not authorise the recipient.
explain_wrong.B: The date establishes when the requisition was made but does not link to authority.
explain_wrong.C: GPhC number identifies a registrant but is not the legal authorising element on a requisition.
explain_wrong.D: A name identifies but does not authorise.
explain_wrong.E: Profession establishes the class of authorised recipient but does not provide individual authorisation.
explain_wrong.F: Purpose explains why stock is wanted but does not link to the recipient's authority.
explain_wrong.G: Quantity describes the request but does not authorise it.

stem: Select the requirement that must be stated on the requisition to justify why the stock is being obtained.
explain_correct: 'Purpose of requisition' is a mandatory legal field on the FP10CDF requisition form. It records why the CD stock is being obtained (e.g. for use in a hospital ward, GP practice, palliative care kit, etc.) and is part of the audit trail to prevent diversion.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001, Regulation 14. FP10CDF requisition form. MEP Edition 45, section 3.6.5.

explain_wrong.A: Address is the recipient's location, not the justification for obtaining stock.
explain_wrong.B: Date records when, not why.
explain_wrong.C: GPhC number identifies the requestor (where applicable) but does not justify the purpose.
explain_wrong.D: Name identifies the requestor, not the reason for the supply.
explain_wrong.E: Profession identifies the type of person, not why the stock is needed.
explain_wrong.G: Quantity is what is obtained, not why.
explain_wrong.H: Signature authorises the requisition but does not state the purpose.

stem:
Patient requests diazepam (Schedule 4 Part I) while on holiday.

Select the maximum quantity that can be supplied in this scenario.
explain_correct: For an emergency supply at the patient's request, Schedule 4 and 5 CDs (and phenobarbital for epilepsy) are limited to a maximum of 5 days' treatment. Diazepam is Schedule 4 Part I, so the 5-day cap applies.
citation: Human Medicines Regulations 2012, Regulation 225(3). MEP Edition 45, section 3.3.10.

explain_wrong.A: 3 days is more restrictive than the legal allowance for Schedule 4 emergency supply.
explain_wrong.C: 7 days exceeds the 5-day cap for CD emergency supply.
explain_wrong.D: 14 days exceeds the 5-day cap.
explain_wrong.E: 28 days is the validity period of CD prescriptions, not an emergency supply quantity.
explain_wrong.F: 30 days is the standard maximum for non-CD POMs in emergency supply; CDs are capped at 5 days.
explain_wrong.G: Full cycle applies to oral contraceptives, not to CDs.
explain_wrong.H: Smallest pack applies to insulin, inhalers, ointments — not to CDs.

stem:
Patient requests Microgynon 30 (oral contraceptive).

Select the maximum quantity that can be supplied in this scenario.
explain_correct: For an emergency supply at the patient's request of an oral contraceptive, a full treatment cycle should be supplied (typically 21 or 28 tablets). The standard 30-day general cap does not apply because the cycle structure is what matters clinically.
citation: Human Medicines Regulations 2012, Regulation 225. MEP Edition 45, section 3.3.10: 'If the POM is an oral contraceptive, a full treatment cycle should be supplied.'

explain_wrong.A: 3 days is for phenobarbital/Schedule 4-5 CDs, not oral contraceptives.
explain_wrong.B: 5 days is the CD emergency supply cap, not the contraceptive cycle.
explain_wrong.C: 7 days does not match either rule.
explain_wrong.D: 14 days does not match either rule.
explain_wrong.E: 28 days is one form of cycle length; the rule is to supply a full cycle, which may be 21 or 28 days depending on the product.
explain_wrong.F: While the standard limit is 30 days for non-CD POMs, oral contraceptives are exempt and supplied as a full cycle.

stem: Select the medication from the list that is legally required to be kept under safe custody.
explain_correct: Buprenorphine is a Schedule 3 CD that IS subject to safe custody requirements, alongside temazepam, diethylpropion, and flunitrazepam. Most other Schedule 3 CDs are exempt.
citation: Misuse of Drugs (Safe Custody) Regulations 1973, Schedule 1. MEP Edition 45, section 3.6.9.

explain_wrong.B: Diethylpropion is also subject to safe custody, but the question asks for ONE answer; buprenorphine is the standard textbook answer for this question's scenario.
explain_wrong.C: Gabapentin is a Schedule 3 CD exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.D: Mazindol is a Schedule 3 CD exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.E: Midazolam is specifically exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.F: Phenobarbital is a Schedule 3 CD exempt from safe custody.
explain_wrong.G: Temazepam is also subject to safe custody (one of the four named exceptions), but buprenorphine is the textbook answer chosen here.

stem: Select the medication from the list that is specifically exempted from the Misuse of Drugs (Safe Custody) Regulations 1973.
explain_correct: Phenobarbital is a Schedule 3 CD that is specifically exempt from the Misuse of Drugs (Safe Custody) Regulations 1973. It does not have to be stored in the CD cabinet (although best practice in many pharmacies is still to do so).
citation: Misuse of Drugs (Safe Custody) Regulations 1973, Schedule 1. MEP Edition 45, section 3.6.9.

explain_wrong.A: Buprenorphine is NOT exempt; it is one of the four Schedule 3 CDs subject to safe custody.
explain_wrong.B: Diethylpropion is NOT exempt; subject to safe custody.
explain_wrong.C: Gabapentin is exempt, but the textbook answer for this question is phenobarbital.
explain_wrong.D: Mazindol is exempt, but the textbook answer is phenobarbital.
explain_wrong.E: Midazolam is exempt, but the textbook answer is phenobarbital.
explain_wrong.G: Temazepam is NOT exempt; it is subject to safe custody.
explain_wrong.H: Tramadol is exempt, but the textbook answer is phenobarbital.

stem:
RP leaves for a 90-minute lunch break.

Which is the most appropriate legal requirement for the RP record regarding this absence?

options.label: A
options.text: Appoint a pharmacy technician as the temporary RP

options.label: B
options.text: No entry is required if the absence is less than 2 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Remain the RP but record the start and end times of the absence

options.label: D
options.text: Sign out of the RP register at 13:00 and sign back in at 14:30

options.label: E
options.text: Update the record only if another pharmacist takes over during the break

explain_correct: Temporary absences of up to 2 hours require the RP to record specific times of absence. the RP remains responsible and must record the time they left and returned.
citation: MEP Edition 45 (RPS, 2022)

explain_wrong.A: Illegal; only a registered pharmacist can be an RP.
explain_wrong.B: the fact of the absence must always be recorded.
explain_wrong.D: signing out terminates 'Responsible Pharmacist' status for the premises.
explain_wrong.E: the current RP must record their own absence.

stem:
First RP is absent for 2 hours; second RP takes over.

What is the maximum remaining time that the second pharmacist can be legally absent as the RP?
explain_correct: The 2-hour absence allowance under the Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008 applies to each individual RP during their period of responsibility, not as a cumulative cap on the pharmacy itself. When a second RP takes over, that pharmacist starts a fresh period of responsibility with a fresh 2-hour absence allowance.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008, Regulation 5. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: 1 hour implies the limit is shared between RPs, which is not the case.
explain_wrong.C: 4 hours is not the legal allowance for a single period of responsibility.
explain_wrong.D: The second pharmacist has their own full 2-hour allowance; 'no time' is incorrect.
explain_wrong.E: Cumulative absence for the premises is not a legal constraint for individual RPs.

stem:
Prescription for methotrexate states 'Take five tablets daily' for arthritis.

According to MHRA safety guidance, which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Change the label to 'weekly' and dispense

options.label: B
options.text: Check the patient's most recent blood test status

options.label: D
options.text: Dispense the medication as prescribed and provide a methotrexate patient card

options.label: E
options.text: Verify the intended weekly dosing with the prescriber

explain_correct: MHRA safety alerts for methotrexate emphasise that for autoimmune conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis), the dose is once WEEKLY, not daily. Daily dosing has caused serious harm and deaths. Where a prescription appears to specify daily dosing for arthritis, the pharmacist must verify the intended weekly schedule with the prescriber before supply.
citation: MHRA / NPSA Safety Alerts on methotrexate. BNF methotrexate monograph. MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.A: Changing the label from 'daily' to 'weekly' without prescriber authorisation is unlawful — pharmacists cannot alter dose frequency unilaterally.
explain_wrong.B: Checking blood test status is part of routine methotrexate monitoring but does not address the immediate prescribing-error risk.
explain_wrong.C: Dispensing a 7-day supply of daily methotrexate would still result in 7 daily doses — the dosing frequency is the safety issue.
explain_wrong.D: Dispensing daily methotrexate for arthritis is a critical safety error; the patient card does not correct an unsafe dosing schedule.

stem:
Patient on ciprofloxacin reports heel pain.

Based on MHRA safety warnings for quinolone antibiotics, which advice should the pharmacist prioritize?

options.label: A
options.text: Avoid dairy products for the remainder of the course

options.label: B
options.text: Continue the course but use a cold compress on the heel

options.label: C
options.text: Stop taking the medication immediately and seek an urgent medical review

options.label: D
options.text: Take the medication with a full glass of water to prevent crystals

options.label: E
options.text: This is a common side effect that will resolve once the course is finished

explain_correct: MHRA safety guidance for fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, etc.) requires immediate cessation of treatment at the first sign of tendon pain, swelling or inflammation. There is a known risk of tendinitis and tendon rupture (particularly Achilles), which can be permanent. The patient should stop the medicine and seek urgent medical review.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update on fluoroquinolones (March 2019, updated). BNF fluoroquinolone class summary.

explain_wrong.A: Avoiding dairy is a separation rule for absorption (cipro chelates with cations), not the response to suspected tendon injury.
explain_wrong.B: Continuing the course with a cold compress ignores the risk of tendon rupture and is contrary to MHRA safety advice.
explain_wrong.D: Hydration to prevent crystalluria is general fluoroquinolone advice but does not address tendon risk.
explain_wrong.E: Dismissing tendon pain as a side effect that will resolve can lead to permanent disability through tendon rupture.

stem: Which information must be included on the mandatory Responsible Pharmacist notice?

options.label: A
options.text: GPhC registration number of the pharmacist

options.label: B
options.text: Name of the pharmacist in charge of the business

explain_correct: Under the Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008, the RP notice displayed in the pharmacy must include the RP's name AND GPhC registration number AND a statement that the named pharmacist is in charge of the pharmacy. The question's correct answer is the name of the pharmacist in charge, which is the most fundamental identifying element on the notice; the GPhC number is also required and is listed alongside.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008, Regulation 7. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: GPhC registration number IS in fact also required on the RP notice. Both name and registration number appear together. The question asks which information must be included, with 'name of the pharmacist' being the principal answer — but A is also a legal element of the notice.
explain_wrong.C: Owner's name is not required on the RP notice.
explain_wrong.D: Staff count is not a notice requirement.
explain_wrong.E: Superintendent's home phone number is personal information and not a notice requirement.

stem:
Prescription for one adrenaline auto-injector for a nut allergy.

According to MHRA safety advice, which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Dispense only the single device as prescribed

options.label: B
options.text: Ensure the device is kept refrigerated at all times

options.label: C
options.text: Recommend the prescriber provides two devices for the patient to carry at all times

options.label: D
options.text: Tell the parent that one device is sufficient for school use

options.label: E
options.text: Use an antihistamine as the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis

explain_correct: MHRA safety advice (following review of adrenaline auto-injectors) recommends that patients at risk of anaphylaxis are prescribed and carry TWO auto-injectors at all times. This is because a second dose may be needed if symptoms do not resolve, if the first device misfires, or while waiting for emergency services.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update on adrenaline auto-injectors (August 2017). Resuscitation Council UK guidance.

explain_wrong.A: Single device dispensing does not match MHRA safety advice; the pharmacist should recommend the prescriber issue two.
explain_wrong.B: Auto-injectors are stored at room temperature, not refrigerated. Refrigeration can damage the device.
explain_wrong.D: One device for school use is insufficient; two should be available wherever the patient is.
explain_wrong.E: Antihistamines are NOT first-line for anaphylaxis. Adrenaline is the life-saving first-line treatment.

stem: Which statement correctly reflects the Responsible Pharmacist's legal duty regarding Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)?

options.label: A
options.text: Ensure that pharmacy procedures are established and maintained

options.label: B
options.text: Only SOPs for Controlled Drugs must be reviewed annually

options.label: C
options.text: Review every SOP personally every 5 years

options.label: D
options.text: The RP must be the author of all pharmacy SOPs

options.label: E
options.text: The Superintendent Pharmacist holds sole legal responsibility for maintaining SOPs

explain_correct: Under the Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008, the RP has a specific legal duty to ensure that pharmacy procedures (SOPs) are established, maintained, and followed. The RP does not need to author every SOP personally but is responsible for ensuring they exist and are appropriate.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.B: All SOPs (not just CD ones) should be reviewed periodically as part of clinical governance, although there is no statutory annual review for CDs specifically.
explain_wrong.C: The RP does not have to review every SOP personally every 5 years — this is not a statutory requirement.
explain_wrong.D: The RP does not have to be the author of every SOP. Authorship can be delegated; responsibility for ensuring SOPs are in place rests with the RP.
explain_wrong.E: The Superintendent Pharmacist has overarching responsibility but the RP has a specific duty to ensure SOPs are maintained in the pharmacy on the day.

stem:
Patient on dapagliflozin reports malaise/nausea with normal glucose.

Which MHRA safety risk should the pharmacist consider as a priority?
explain_correct: MHRA Drug Safety Update on SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin) warns that these medicines can cause diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) at near-normal glucose levels — known as 'euglycaemic DKA'. The presentation can be misleading because the patient's glucose may not be markedly elevated. Symptoms of nausea, vomiting, malaise, and abdominal pain in a patient on an SGLT2 inhibitor should prompt ketone testing regardless of glucose level.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update on SGLT2 inhibitors (April 2016, June 2020). BNF SGLT2 inhibitor class summary.

explain_wrong.A: AKI is a known risk but is not the specific MHRA-flagged hidden danger that euglycaemic DKA represents.
explain_wrong.B: B12 deficiency is associated with metformin (not SGLT2 inhibitors).
explain_wrong.D: Hypoglycaemia is uncommon with SGLT2 inhibitor monotherapy. Euglycaemia makes DKA the hidden danger here.
explain_wrong.E: UTI is more common with SGLT2 inhibitors due to glycosuria, but the MHRA priority for these specific symptoms (malaise, nausea, normal glucose) is euglycaemic DKA.

topic_detail: Responsible Pharmacist - Retail Sale of GSL
stem:
Pharmacy closed; supermarket remains open.

Which is the most appropriate legal restriction regarding the sale of medicines after the RP signs out?

options.label: A
options.text: Dispensing of repeat prescriptions can continue

options.label: B
options.text: GSL medicines can continue to be sold in the supermarket area

options.label: C
options.text: No medicines of any category can be sold on the premises

options.label: D
options.text: Only emergency supplies can be made by a senior technician

options.label: E
options.text: P medicines can be sold if the supermarket manager supervises the sale

explain_correct: General Sale List (GSL) medicines can be sold from non-pharmacy retail outlets (such as the supermarket area) without a pharmacist or Responsible Pharmacist on duty, provided the conditions for GSL sale are met (suitable retail premises, sealed containers, etc.). When the pharmacy itself is closed and the RP has signed out, the supermarket can continue selling GSL medicines.
citation: Medicines Act 1968 (GSL sales). Human Medicines Regulations 2012. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: Dispensing of prescriptions cannot continue without the RP — clinical and accuracy checks require pharmacist presence.
explain_wrong.C: GSL sales CAN continue from non-pharmacy retail premises without an RP.
explain_wrong.D: Emergency supplies require a pharmacist to assess and supply; a senior technician cannot make emergency supplies.
explain_wrong.E: P medicine sales require pharmacist supervision; a supermarket manager cannot supervise P sales.

stem:
Counseling for Prolia (denosumab) injection.

Which MHRA safety monitoring requirement is most important to emphasize?

options.label: A
options.text: Avoid all sunlight while taking this medication

options.label: C
options.text: Hair loss is a sign of medication toxicity

options.label: E
options.text: Report any new or unusual thigh, hip, or groin pain

explain_correct: MHRA safety guidance on denosumab (Prolia, Xgeva) highlights the risk of atypical femoral fractures. Patients should be advised to report any new or unusual thigh, hip or groin pain promptly, as this may be an early sign of an atypical fracture. Counselling on this risk is the most important monitoring/safety message.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update on denosumab (atypical femoral fractures). MEP Edition 45 Drug Safety section.

explain_wrong.A: There is no MHRA advice to avoid sunlight while taking denosumab.
explain_wrong.B: While good hydration is generally advisable, a daily 2L water intake is not the specific MHRA monitoring message for denosumab.
explain_wrong.C: Hair loss is not a recognised MHRA-flagged adverse effect of denosumab requiring specific patient counselling.
explain_wrong.D: Daily morning blood pressure monitoring is not the MHRA-specific safety counselling for denosumab.

stem:
Heart failure patient prescribed hydroxyzine.

Which MHRA safety precaution is most relevant to this patient?

options.label: B
options.text: Hydroxyzine is contraindicated in patients with heart failure

options.label: C
options.text: Maximum daily dose is 100mg for adults

options.label: D
options.text: Stop taking 48 hours before any allergy test

options.label: E
options.text: Take with food to avoid gastric irritation

explain_correct: MHRA safety advice (Drug Safety Update April 2015) restricted the use of hydroxyzine due to QT prolongation risk. Hydroxyzine is now contraindicated in patients with risk factors for QT prolongation, including those with significant cardiac conditions such as heart failure.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update April 2015 (hydroxyzine and QT prolongation). MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.A: Hydroxyzine is not associated with severe rebound hypertension.
explain_wrong.C: There is a maximum daily dose for hydroxyzine in adults (100 mg, but reduced in older patients), but this is not the safety precaution most relevant to a heart failure patient — the contraindication is.
explain_wrong.D: Stopping antihistamines before allergy testing is general allergy-test advice, not the heart failure-specific MHRA safety precaution.
explain_wrong.E: Taking with food to avoid gastric irritation is not relevant to the cardiac contraindication.

stem:
Handing out pre-checked blister packs during RP absence.

Can the pharmacy technician hand out these checked blister packs to the patients while the RP is absent?

options.label: A
options.text: No, POM handover is a professional activity

options.label: B
options.text: No, a second pharmacist is needed to witness the handover

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, because the clinical and accuracy checks are complete

options.label: D
options.text: Yes, if the RP has specifically authorized the activity and a record is made

options.label: E
options.text: Yes, but only for repeat patients

explain_correct: Pre-checked blister packs (i.e. clinical and accuracy checks already done by the pharmacist) can be handed out by trained pharmacy staff during the Responsible Pharmacist's absence, but only if the RP has specifically authorised the activity in advance and a record is kept. This is permitted under the Responsible Pharmacist Regulations 2008.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: Handover of a pre-checked POM is not a professional activity that requires pharmacist presence, provided the RP has authorised the activity and a record is made.
explain_wrong.B: A second pharmacist is not required to witness the handover of a pre-checked medicine.
explain_wrong.C: Although the clinical and accuracy checks are complete, RP authorisation and a record are still required. The completion of the checks is necessary but not sufficient.
explain_wrong.E: There is no rule limiting handover to repeat patients. The RP authorisation requirement applies to all patients.

stem:
Valproate patient using progestogen-only pill (POP).

Which advice is most appropriate regarding her contraception according to the PPP?

options.label: A
options.text: Only use contraception if sexually active

options.label: B
options.text: The progestogen-only pill is considered highly effective

options.label: C
options.text: The POP is not 'highly effective'; a Long-Acting Reversible Contraceptive (LARC) is required

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a combined oral contraceptive pill

options.label: E
options.text: Use two forms of contraception including one barrier method

explain_correct: Under the MHRA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP), patients of childbearing potential must use 'highly effective' contraception. The progestogen-only pill is user-dependent and is NOT considered 'highly effective' for PPP purposes. A Long-Acting Reversible Contraceptive (LARC) such as an implant, IUS or IUD is required, or the patient must use two complementary methods.
citation: MHRA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme (April 2018, updated). MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.A: Contraception under PPP is required for women of childbearing potential regardless of current sexual activity, because of the high risk of fetal harm if pregnancy occurs unexpectedly.
explain_wrong.B: The POP is not classed as 'highly effective' under PPP definitions.
explain_wrong.D: The combined oral contraceptive pill is also not 'highly effective' under PPP definitions because it is user-dependent. LARC is the answer.
explain_wrong.E: Two forms with one barrier method is one acceptable alternative under PPP, but the simpler and more clearly 'highly effective' option (and the standard PPP recommendation) is a LARC.

stem: For how long must the Responsible Pharmacist record be retained?
explain_correct: The Responsible Pharmacist record must be retained for at least 5 years. The RP record contains the date and time of starting and ending the period of responsibility, the RP's name, signature, and registration number, and any changes during the period.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008, Regulation 8. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: 1 year is too short. The legal minimum retention is 5 years.
explain_wrong.B: 2 years is the POM register retention period, not the RP record retention.
explain_wrong.D: 6 years is not the legal RP record retention period.
explain_wrong.E: Permanent retention is not required by law, although organisations may choose to keep records longer.

stem:
Cardiac patient seeking OTC remedy for nausea.

Select the medication restricted to POM-only and contraindicated in cardiac conditions.
explain_correct: Domperidone was restricted to POM-only following MHRA Drug Safety Update advice (April 2014, updated 2019) due to a small increased risk of serious cardiac side effects, including QT prolongation and cardiac arrest. It is contraindicated in patients with underlying cardiac conditions.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update on domperidone (cardiac risk). MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.B: Hydroxyzine has cardiac contraindications too but is a POM antihistamine, not a typical OTC-restricted antiemetic. The question specifically refers to OTC antiemetic context.
explain_wrong.C: Isotretinoin is a POM dermatology drug, not an antiemetic.
explain_wrong.D: Methotrexate is a cytotoxic/immunosuppressant, not an antiemetic.
explain_wrong.E: Modafinil is a wakefulness-promoting agent, not an antiemetic.
explain_wrong.F: Oral contraceptives are not antiemetics.
explain_wrong.G: Valproate is an antiepileptic with severe pregnancy risks; not an antiemetic.
explain_wrong.H: Warfarin is an anticoagulant; not an antiemetic.

stem:
Narcolepsy patient on oral contraception starting new med.

Select the medication that reduces hormonal contraceptive effectiveness.
explain_correct: Modafinil is an enzyme inducer that reduces the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives. Patients on modafinil who use hormonal contraception must use additional or alternative contraception during treatment and for at least 2 months after stopping.
citation: MHRA Drug Safety Update on modafinil (interaction with hormonal contraceptives). BNF / SmPC.

explain_wrong.A: Domperidone does not significantly induce contraceptive metabolism.
explain_wrong.B: Hydroxyzine is not a contraceptive enzyme inducer.
explain_wrong.C: Isotretinoin requires highly effective contraception in women of childbearing potential because of teratogenicity, but it does not reduce the effectiveness of hormonal contraception.
explain_wrong.D: Methotrexate has fertility/teratogenicity warnings but is not a contraceptive enzyme inducer.
explain_wrong.F: Oral contraceptive itself is the medicine being affected, not the inducer.
explain_wrong.G: Valproate has teratogenicity risks (PPP) but is not a contraceptive enzyme inducer.
explain_wrong.H: Warfarin does not reduce the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives.

stem:
Male starting isotretinoin for acne.

Select mandatory test to monitor hepatotoxicity.
explain_correct: Isotretinoin can cause hepatotoxicity (raised liver enzymes). Liver function tests (LFTs) are mandatory before starting treatment and must be monitored during treatment. Lipid profile is also typically monitored alongside.
citation: Isotretinoin SmPC. MHRA Drug Safety Updates. BNF.

explain_wrong.A: Bone mineral density is not a standard isotretinoin monitoring test.
explain_wrong.B: FBC is not the specific test for hepatotoxicity.
explain_wrong.C: Glucose monitoring is not a routine isotretinoin requirement.
explain_wrong.E: Monthly pregnancy testing is mandatory under the isotretinoin PPP for women of childbearing potential, but the question asks about hepatotoxicity, not teratogenicity. (For a male patient, pregnancy testing is not relevant.)
explain_wrong.F: Thyroid function is not the hepatotoxicity test.
explain_wrong.G: U&Es assess renal function, not hepatic.
explain_wrong.H: Visual acuity is not the hepatotoxicity test.

stem:
Man on amiodarone.

Select test for side effect affecting metabolism every 6 months.
explain_correct: Amiodarone can cause both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism due to its high iodine content. Thyroid function tests (TFTs) must be checked before initiation and every 6 months during treatment. Liver function and pulmonary function are also routinely monitored.
citation: Amiodarone SmPC. BNF amiodarone monograph. MHRA monitoring guidance.

explain_wrong.A: Bone mineral density is not the metabolism-related amiodarone monitoring test.
explain_wrong.B: FBC is not a routine amiodarone monitoring requirement.
explain_wrong.C: Glucose is not the amiodarone-specific monitoring test.
explain_wrong.D: LFTs are also monitored on amiodarone (every 6 months), but the question specifies the test for the side effect 'affecting metabolism' — that is thyroid function.
explain_wrong.E: Pregnancy testing is not the amiodarone metabolism monitor.
explain_wrong.G: U&Es are not the metabolism-related amiodarone test.
explain_wrong.H: Visual acuity is monitored for some drugs (e.g. ethambutol) but not as the metabolism marker for amiodarone.

stem: Select the activity strictly prohibited while the RP is absent.

options.label: A
options.text: Accuracy check of a prescribed item

options.label: D
options.text: Handover of a checked POM to a patient

explain_correct: Sale of P (Pharmacy-only) medicines requires the Responsible Pharmacist to be present and supervising the transaction. P medicine sales cannot take place during the RP's absence. Other professional activities like clinical and accuracy checks are also pharmacist activities, but the question asks specifically about activities prohibited during RP absence — sale of P medicines is the standard exam answer because it is the most clearly defined prohibition during the up-to-2-hour absence window.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008. Medicines Act 1968 (P medicine supervision). MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: Accuracy checks are professional pharmacist activities and require pharmacist presence, but in some pharmacies an Accuracy Checking Technician (ACT) can carry out accuracy checks. The question asks for the activity strictly prohibited; sale of P medicines is the more clear-cut prohibition.
explain_wrong.B: Assembly (i.e. labelling and packaging of medicines) can take place during RP absence. It is not a professional supervised activity.
explain_wrong.C: Clinical check requires a pharmacist (cannot be delegated), but it is permitted only when a pharmacist is present — and another pharmacist could perform this. The most universally prohibited activity during RP absence is sale of P medicines.
explain_wrong.D: Handover of pre-checked POMs to patients can take place during RP absence if specifically authorised by the RP and recorded.
explain_wrong.E: Making a CD register entry is an administrative record-keeping task and can be done during RP absence.
explain_wrong.F: Processing a private prescription (administration, payment, etc.) can be done during RP absence; the clinical and accuracy checks must be done when a pharmacist is present.
explain_wrong.H: Wholesale distribution can occur during RP absence under appropriate procedures.

stem: Select activity technician can legally perform during RP absence.

options.label: A
options.text: Accuracy check of a prescribed item

options.label: D
options.text: Handover of a checked POM to a patient

explain_correct: Assembly (preparation, labelling, packaging) of medicinal products is not a professional supervised activity and can be carried out by trained staff during the Responsible Pharmacist's absence. The sale of P medicines and the professional check (clinical and accuracy) require the pharmacist to be present.
citation: Pharmacy (Responsible Pharmacists) Regulations 2008. MEP Edition 45, section 3.2.1.

explain_wrong.A: Accuracy check is a pharmacist activity. While an Accuracy Checking Technician (ACT) can perform it, the standard position is that the accuracy check requires pharmacist presence or ACT under appropriate governance.
explain_wrong.C: Clinical check must be done by a pharmacist and cannot be delegated to a technician.
explain_wrong.D: Handover of a checked POM during RP absence requires specific RP authorisation and a record. It is not a routine technician activity without such authorisation.
explain_wrong.E: Making a CD register entry is administrative and can occur during RP absence (so this answer would also be acceptable from a strict reading), but the question asks for the activity a technician can legally perform, and assembly is the clearer textbook answer.
explain_wrong.F: Processing a private prescription (i.e. completing the dispensing process) requires the clinical and accuracy checks to be done while a pharmacist is present.
explain_wrong.G: Sale of P medicines is the textbook prohibition during RP absence.
explain_wrong.H: Wholesale distribution can occur but is governed by separate WDA requirements; it is not the standard 'technician during RP absence' activity.

stem: What is the most appropriate next step if a medicine authorized in the UK for another animal species or another condition in the same species is unavailable?

options.label: B
options.text: A medicine authorized in the UK for human use

options.label: C
options.text: A veterinary medicine authorized in a country outside the UK

options.label: D
options.text: A veterinary medicine authorized in the UK for a different condition

options.label: E
options.text: A veterinary medicine authorized in the UK for a different species

explain_correct: The Veterinary Cascade has four tiers: (1) UK-licensed veterinary medicine for the species and condition, (2) UK-licensed veterinary medicine for another species or for another condition in the same species, (3) UK-licensed human medicine OR veterinary medicine authorised outside the UK, and (4) extemporaneously prepared / specially manufactured medicine. If Tier 2 (vet medicine for different species or condition) is unavailable, the vet moves to Tier 3 — a UK-authorised human medicine being one of the options.
citation: Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013. MEP Edition 45, section 3.5.2.

explain_wrong.A: An extemporaneous preparation is Tier 4, the last resort. The vet should consider Tier 3 (UK-licensed human medicine) before extemporaneous preparation.
explain_wrong.C: A non-UK veterinary medicine is part of Tier 3 alongside UK-licensed human medicines, but the question asks for the most appropriate next step. UK-licensed human medicines are typically available and considered first within Tier 3.
explain_wrong.D: A vet medicine for a different condition is part of Tier 2, which the question states is unavailable.
explain_wrong.E: A vet medicine for a different species is part of Tier 2, which the question states is unavailable.

stem:
Phenobarbital RX for dog dated 1 May; presented 30 May.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Contact the veterinarian to verify the clinical need

options.label: B
options.text: Dispense the medication as veterinary prescriptions are valid for 6 months

options.label: C
options.text: Dispense the medication but inform the owner no repeats are allowed

options.label: D
options.text: Record the details in the POM-V register before dispensing

options.label: E
options.text: Refuse to dispense the medication as the prescription is expired

explain_correct: Veterinary prescriptions for Schedule 2, 3 and 4 CDs are valid for 28 days from the appropriate date — i.e. valid up to and including (appropriate date + 28). For a prescription dated 1 May, the last valid day is 29 May. Presentation on 30 May is past validity. The pharmacist must refuse to dispense and the owner must obtain a fresh prescription.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001, Regulation 15. MEP Edition 45, section 3.5.1.

explain_wrong.A: Contacting the vet to verify clinical need does not bring an expired CD prescription back into validity. A new prescription is required.
explain_wrong.B: The 6-month validity applies to non-CD veterinary POMs (POM-V), not to CD prescriptions. Phenobarbital is a Schedule 3 CD and the 28-day validity applies.
explain_wrong.C: Repeats are not the issue; the prescription has expired entirely.
explain_wrong.D: Recording in POM-V register before dispensing does not validate an expired CD prescription.

stem: Which is the most appropriate legal requirement for the pharmacist to fulfill before completing the sale of a Schedule 1 poison?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a label stating 'Poison - Not to be taken'

options.label: B
options.text: Check the purchaser's passport for identity verification

options.label: C
options.text: Record the sale and obtain the purchaser's signature in the Poisons Book

options.label: D
options.text: Verify the purchaser's Home Office licence for regulated substances

options.label: E
options.text: Wholesale distribution rules apply; no retail sale is permitted

explain_correct: Under the Poisons Act 1972 (as amended) and Poisons Regulations, sales of regulated substances (the modern equivalent of the old Schedule 1 poisons category) must be recorded in the pharmacy's Poisons Book at the point of sale, with the purchaser's signature, before the sale is completed.
citation: Poisons Act 1972 (as amended by the Deregulation Act 2015 and Poisons and Explosives Precursors Regulations 2015). MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.A: While poison products are labelled 'Poison', applying that label is not the legal requirement for the pharmacist to fulfil before sale; recording in the Poisons Book is.
explain_wrong.B: Passport identity verification is not the standard legal requirement; the regulated-substance sale requires Home Office EPP licence verification (for regulated substances) and signature in the Poisons Book.
explain_wrong.D: For 'regulated substances' (formerly Schedule 1 poisons listed as precursor chemicals), Home Office EPP licence verification is required; for 'reportable substances' (formerly other Schedule 1), licence is not required but record-keeping is. The most appropriate step before completing any Sch 1 sale is the Poisons Book entry.
explain_wrong.E: Schedule 1/regulated poisons can be sold at retail to licensed purchasers; this is not a wholesale-only category.

stem: Which statement correctly reflects the legal requirement for recording the use of Industrial Denatured Alcohol (IDA) in a pharmacy?

options.label: A
options.text: A report must be submitted to the Home Office annually

options.label: B
options.text: No records are required if the quantity is less than 5 litres

options.label: C
options.text: No records are required for IDA use in medicines

options.label: D
options.text: Only the sale of IDA as a standalone product must be recorded

options.label: E
options.text: Records of all receipts and use of IDA must be kept for HMRC inspection

explain_correct: Authorised users of Industrial Denatured Alcohol (IDA) must keep records of all receipts and use of IDA, available for HMRC inspection. IDA is duty-free spirit denatured for industrial/laboratory use, and HMRC controls its movement and use to prevent diversion to drinkable alcohol.
citation: Alcoholic Liquor Duties Act 1979. HMRC Notice 473 (Production, distribution and use of denatured alcohol). MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.A: There is no annual Home Office report for IDA. HMRC, not the Home Office, regulates denatured alcohol, and records are kept for inspection rather than submitted annually.
explain_wrong.B: There is no 5-litre exemption from record-keeping. All receipts and use must be recorded.
explain_wrong.C: Records must be kept for IDA used in any context, including in medicines preparation.
explain_wrong.D: Records are not limited to standalone product sale. All receipts and use of IDA must be recorded.

stem:
Patient struggles to open medication bottles.

Under GPhC Standard 1 (Person-centred care), which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist?

options.label: A
options.text: Advise the patient to have a family member open the bottles

options.label: B
options.text: Document the patient's difficulty and take no further action

options.label: C
options.text: Explain the availability of wing-caps or easy-open tops and offer to switch

options.label: D
options.text: Inform the GP that the patient is likely to be non-compliant

options.label: E
options.text: Tell the patient they must be more assertive when requesting help

explain_correct: GPhC Standard 1 (Person-centred care) requires pharmacy professionals to identify and address barriers to access. Offering an alternative cap (e.g. wing-cap or easy-open top) and arranging the change directly addresses the patient's difficulty in a person-centred way.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standard 1.

explain_wrong.A: Telling the patient to find a family member shifts the burden to the patient and does not address the access barrier through the pharmacy's own resources.
explain_wrong.B: Documenting and taking no further action is the opposite of person-centred care; the standard requires action to remove the barrier.
explain_wrong.D: Informing the GP that the patient is non-compliant labels the patient negatively and ignores the actual barrier (which is the cap design, not the patient's behaviour).
explain_wrong.E: Telling the patient to be more assertive blames the patient and fails to provide a solution.

stem:
Pharmacist multi-tasks during OTC counseling.

Which GPhC professional standard is most likely to have been failed in this scenario?
explain_correct: GPhC Standard 3 (Communicate effectively) requires pharmacy professionals to give the patient their full attention and use language the patient can understand. Multitasking during a counselling interaction undermines the quality of communication and is the standard most directly compromised here.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standard 3.

explain_wrong.A: Standard 1 (Person-centred care) covers tailoring care to the individual but is broader; the specific failure here is in communication quality, which is Standard 3.
explain_wrong.C: Standard 5 (Use professional judgement) concerns clinical and professional decision-making, not the quality of attention given during a conversation.
explain_wrong.D: Standard 6 (Behave professionally) covers conduct and integrity. While multitasking could touch on professionalism, the specific failure is in the communication itself.
explain_wrong.E: Standard 9 (Demonstrate leadership) is about influence and modelling good practice, not about attention during individual patient interactions.

stem:
Dispensing error identified; wrong strength supplied.

Which is the most appropriate action to comply with the professional duty of candour?

options.label: A
options.text: Inform the GP and ask them to manage the patient interaction

options.label: B
options.text: Inform the patient of the error, apologize, and provide the correct medication

options.label: C
options.text: Make a quiet correction and say nothing to the patient since no harm occurred

options.label: D
options.text: Record the incident in the near-miss log only

options.label: E
options.text: Wait for the patient to return and notice the error themselves

explain_correct: The professional duty of candour requires the pharmacist to be open and honest with the patient when something has gone wrong, regardless of whether actual harm occurred. The correct response is to tell the patient, apologise, supply the correct medicine, and follow the pharmacy's incident reporting procedure. GPhC Standards 6 (behave professionally) and 8 (speak up) underpin this.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standards 6 and 8. Duty of candour (statutory and professional). MEP Edition 45, section 1.7.

explain_wrong.A: Asking the GP to manage the patient interaction does not satisfy the pharmacist's own duty of candour. The pharmacist who made the error is responsible for being open with the patient.
explain_wrong.C: Quietly correcting and saying nothing breaches the duty of candour. The duty applies regardless of whether harm occurred.
explain_wrong.D: Recording in the near-miss log is necessary internally but is not a substitute for telling the patient. Both are required.
explain_wrong.E: Waiting for the patient to notice is dishonest and breaches the duty of candour.

stem: For how long must records of receipt and supply of POM-V medicines be retained?
explain_correct: Records of receipt and supply of POM-V (and POM-VPS) medicines must be retained for at least 5 years. The records must include the name of the medicine, date of receipt or supply, batch number, quantity, name and address of supplier or recipient, and (for supply) the prescription details.
citation: Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013, Schedule 3. MEP Edition 45, section 3.5.4.

explain_wrong.A: 1 year is too short; the legal minimum is 5 years.
explain_wrong.B: 2 years is the human POM register retention period, not the veterinary requirement.
explain_wrong.C: 3 years is not the statutory retention period for veterinary medicine records.
explain_wrong.E: 10 years exceeds the statutory minimum, which is 5 years.

stem: Which is the most appropriate legal restriction for the pharmacist to consider regarding the retail sale of Mineralized Denatured Alcohol (MDA)?

options.label: A
options.text: No quantity restrictions apply to the retail sale of MDA

options.label: B
options.text: The purchaser must be at least 16 years old

options.label: C
options.text: The sale must be recorded in the private prescription book

options.label: D
options.text: The sale of MDA is strictly prohibited in community pharmacy

options.label: E
options.text: The user must provide a valid HMRC permit before purchase

explain_correct: Mineralized Methylated Spirits (MDA, more commonly called 'meths') has no legal quantity restrictions for retail sale. It is freely available without an HMRC permit because the denaturants make it unfit for consumption. Industrial Denatured Alcohol (IDA), by contrast, requires an authorised user.
citation: HMRC Notice 473 (Production, distribution and use of denatured alcohol). Alcoholic Liquor Duties Act 1979.

explain_wrong.B: There is no specific minimum age requirement for purchasing MDA. MDA is not classed as a regulated alcoholic drink.
explain_wrong.C: Recording in the private prescription book is not required for MDA retail sale; MDA is not a prescription medicine.
explain_wrong.D: Sale of MDA from community pharmacies is permitted; it is not prohibited.
explain_wrong.E: An HMRC permit is required by users of IDA, not by purchasers of MDA. MDA is freely sold.

stem:
Patient invites pharmacist to private dinner.

According to GPhC Standard 6 (Maintain professional boundaries), which is the most appropriate response?

options.label: A
options.text: Accept the invitation to build a stronger patient-pharmacist relationship

options.label: B
options.text: Accept only if other members of the pharmacy team also attend

options.label: C
options.text: Ask the patient for a small professional gift instead

options.label: D
options.text: Politely decline the invitation and explain the need for professional boundaries

options.label: E
options.text: Report the patient for inappropriate behaviour

explain_correct: GPhC Standard 6 (Behave professionally) requires pharmacy professionals to maintain appropriate professional boundaries with patients. Accepting a private dinner invitation from a patient blurs the professional relationship and could compromise objectivity. Politely declining and explaining the boundary is the correct response.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standard 6.

explain_wrong.A: Accepting to 'build a stronger relationship' compromises professional boundaries. The pharmacist-patient relationship is professional, not social.
explain_wrong.B: Attending with other team members does not remove the boundary issue. The relationship between the patient and the professionals remains professional regardless of who is present.
explain_wrong.C: Asking for a gift instead is inappropriate and could be construed as soliciting from a patient.
explain_wrong.E: An invitation to dinner is not in itself inappropriate behaviour by the patient. Reporting the patient is disproportionate; politely declining is sufficient.

stem: Select the legal category that applies to a flea treatment sold at any retail outlet.
explain_correct: AVM-GSL (Authorised Veterinary Medicine - General Sales List) products can be sold from any retail outlet without restriction. They do not require a pharmacist, vet or SQP to be involved in the sale. Common AVM-GSL products include some flea and worming products for domestic pets.
citation: Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013. MEP Edition 45, section 3.5.

explain_wrong.B: CD POM is not a veterinary medicine category. Schedule 2 and 3 CDs prescribed for animals follow the same prescription requirements as for humans plus VMR labelling.
explain_wrong.C: NFA-VPS (Non-Food Animal - Veterinarian, Pharmacist, SQP) requires sale by a vet, pharmacist or Suitably Qualified Person and is not unrestricted.
explain_wrong.D: POM-V (Prescription-Only Medicine - Veterinarian) requires a written veterinary prescription and is the most restricted category.
explain_wrong.E: POM-VPS (Prescription-Only Medicine - Veterinarian, Pharmacist, SQP) is for food-producing animals and can be prescribed by a pharmacist or SQP, but it is not sold from any retail outlet without restriction.
explain_wrong.F: SAES (Small Animal Exemption Scheme) covers certain unlicensed medicines for small pet animals; not retail-outlet flea treatments.

stem: Select the legal category that applies to sheep worming products sold by a pharmacist, vet, or SQP without a mandatory clinical assessment.
explain_correct: POM-VPS (Prescription-Only Medicine - Veterinarian, Pharmacist, Suitably Qualified Person) covers products primarily for food-producing animals. They can be prescribed and supplied by a vet, pharmacist, or SQP without a mandatory vet clinical assessment. Sheep wormers fall into this category.
citation: Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013. MEP Edition 45, section 3.5.

explain_wrong.A: AVM-GSL is unrestricted general retail and does not require pharmacist/vet/SQP supply.
explain_wrong.B: CD POM is not a veterinary medicine category.
explain_wrong.C: NFA-VPS (Non-Food Animal) is for non-food animals like pets; sheep are food-producing animals so this does not apply.
explain_wrong.D: POM-V requires a written veterinary prescription based on clinical assessment by the vet. The question specifies no clinical assessment is required, so POM-VPS is the correct category.
explain_wrong.F: SAES is the Small Animal Exemption Scheme for unlicensed medicines for pet animals.

stem: Select the type of denatured alcohol typically authorized for perfume manufacture.
explain_correct: Trade Specific Denatured Alcohol (TSDA) is formulated with denaturants suitable for specific industrial uses such as perfumes, toiletries and cosmetics. HMRC authorises TSDA use under specific formulae for these trades.
citation: HMRC Notice 473 (Production, distribution and use of denatured alcohol).

explain_wrong.A: Completely Denatured Alcohol (CDA) is the freely available 'meths' form, not the perfume-manufacturing form.
explain_wrong.B: Industrial Denatured Alcohol (IDA) is general industrial use under HMRC authorisation but is not the specific category for perfume manufacture; TSDA is.

stem: Select the type of denatured alcohol used for standard 'methylated spirits'.
explain_correct: Completely Denatured Alcohol (CDA) is the statutory term for what is commonly called 'methylated spirits' (meths). It contains a fixed denaturing formula prescribed by HMRC and is freely available for retail sale.
citation: HMRC Notice 473 (Production, distribution and use of denatured alcohol).

explain_wrong.B: Industrial Denatured Alcohol (IDA) is for authorised industrial users with HMRC permits; it is not the standard 'meths' product.
explain_wrong.C: Trade Specific Denatured Alcohol (TSDA) is for specific trades such as perfumery and cosmetics, not standard methylated spirits.

stem:
Colleague appears intoxicated at work.

Select the GPhC professional standard requiring you to speak up about a colleague's fitness to practise.
explain_correct: GPhC Standard 8 (Speak up when you have concerns or things go wrong) requires pharmacy professionals to raise concerns about safety, including concerns about a colleague's fitness to practise. An apparently intoxicated colleague is a clear patient safety concern that must be raised.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standard 8.

explain_wrong.A: Standard 1 (Person-centred care) is about delivering care to individual patients, not about speaking up about colleagues.
explain_wrong.B: Standard 2 (Working in partnership) covers collaboration with the patient, carers, and the wider team but is not the speak-up standard.
explain_wrong.C: Standard 3 (Communicate effectively) covers the quality of communication with patients and colleagues, not the duty to escalate concerns.
explain_wrong.D: Standard 4 (Maintain, develop and use professional knowledge and skills) covers CPD and competence, not the speak-up duty.
explain_wrong.E: Standard 5 (Use professional judgement) covers clinical/professional decision-making, not raising safety concerns about colleagues.
explain_wrong.F: Standard 6 (Behave professionally) is about the individual professional's own conduct.
explain_wrong.G: Standard 7 (Respect and maintain confidentiality and privacy) is about handling patient information.
explain_wrong.I: Standard 9 (Demonstrate leadership) is broader leadership behaviour; the specific speak-up duty is Standard 8.

stem: Select the GPhC professional standard requiring the maintenance of professional knowledge and skills.
explain_correct: GPhC Standard 4 requires pharmacy professionals to maintain, develop and use their professional knowledge and skills. This is the basis for the GPhC's Continuing Professional Development (CPD) and revalidation requirements.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standard 4.

explain_wrong.A: Standard 1 (Person-centred care) is about tailoring care to the patient.
explain_wrong.B: Standard 2 (Working in partnership) is about collaboration.
explain_wrong.C: Standard 3 (Communicate effectively) is about communication quality.
explain_wrong.E: Standard 5 (Use professional judgement) is about decision-making.
explain_wrong.F: Standard 6 (Behave professionally) is about conduct and integrity.
explain_wrong.G: Standard 7 (Respect and maintain confidentiality and privacy).
explain_wrong.H: Standard 8 (Speak up) is about raising concerns.

stem: Which record-keeping action is a legal requirement for morphine tablets (Schedule 2) supplied to a dog?

options.label: A
options.text: An entry in both the Controlled Drugs register and the POM-V register

options.label: B
options.text: An entry in the human Controlled Drugs register only

options.label: C
options.text: An entry in the POM-V register only

options.label: D
options.text: No record is required if the prescription is NHS-funded

options.label: E
options.text: Sending the original prescription to the NHSBSA

explain_correct: Schedule 2 CDs supplied for veterinary use must be entered in the CD register (under the requirements of the Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001) and also in the veterinary POM-V record (under the Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013). Both records are required because the medicine is regulated by both regimes simultaneously.
citation: Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001, Regulation 19 (CD register). Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013, Schedule 3 (POM-V record). MEP Edition 45, sections 3.5 and 3.6.

explain_wrong.B: Recording in the human CD register only is insufficient. The POM-V record under VMR 2013 is also a legal requirement for veterinary medicine receipt and supply.
explain_wrong.C: Recording in the POM-V register only is insufficient for a Schedule 2 CD. The CD register requirement under MDR 2001 also applies.
explain_wrong.D: There is no NHS-funded exemption from CD record keeping. The CD register and POM-V record requirements are not affected by funding source — and in any case, vet prescriptions are not NHS-funded.
explain_wrong.E: Veterinary prescriptions are not submitted to the NHSBSA. They are retained by the dispensing pharmacy for 5 years.

stem: What is the mandatory labelling requirement for the container of a Schedule 1 poison?

options.label: A
options.text: A label stating 'For external use only'

options.label: B
options.text: A label stating 'Keep out of reach of children'

options.label: C
options.text: A label stating the name of the poison and the word 'Poison'

options.label: D
options.text: A label stating the manufacturer's head office address

options.label: E
options.text: A label warning that the product may cause drowsiness

explain_correct: The container of a Schedule 1 / regulated poison must be labelled with the name of the substance and the word 'Poison' (or in some cases the chemical name). This statutory labelling alerts handlers and the public to the hazard.
citation: Poisons Act 1972; Poisons Rules. CLP Regulation (EC) 1272/2008 for hazard pictograms.

explain_wrong.A: 'For external use only' is a labelling requirement for some external-use medicines, not the Schedule 1 poisons label.
explain_wrong.B: 'Keep out of reach of children' is a general medicines labelling requirement, not specific to Schedule 1 poisons.
explain_wrong.D: The manufacturer's head office address is not a Schedule 1 poison labelling requirement.
explain_wrong.E: Drowsiness warnings are for sedating medicines, not poisons.

stem:
Discussion of patient in earshot of public.

Which GPhC standard is most likely to have been breached in this scenario?
explain_correct: GPhC Standard 7 (Respect and maintain a person's confidentiality and privacy) requires pharmacy professionals to ensure patient information is not disclosed inappropriately. Discussing a patient's information in earshot of the public is a clear breach of confidentiality.
citation: GPhC Standards for Pharmacy Professionals (May 2017), Standard 7. GPhC Guidance on confidentiality.

explain_wrong.A: Standard 1 (Person-centred care) is about tailoring care; it is not the standard most directly engaged by a confidentiality breach.
explain_wrong.B: Standard 4 (Professional skills) is about CPD and competence.
explain_wrong.D: Standard 8 (Speak up) is about raising concerns, not about protecting confidentiality.
explain_wrong.E: Standard 9 (Leadership) is about modelling good practice.

stem:
Private prescription for medicinal pure ethanol.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Dispense the ethanol and charge the patient the price including excise duty

options.label: B
options.text: Dispense the ethanol only if it is for a patient in a hospital setting

options.label: C
options.text: Mix the ethanol with a denaturant before dispensing to the patient

options.label: D
options.text: Refuse to dispense as pure ethanol cannot be sold to individuals

options.label: E
options.text: Request a pink FP10PCD form before dispensing

explain_correct: Pharmacists can dispense duty-paid medicinal pure ethanol on a private prescription. The cost passed to the patient includes excise duty. There is no requirement to denature the spirit before dispensing or to use a special prescription form.
citation: Alcoholic Liquor Duties Act 1979. HMRC duty-paid spirits guidance. MEP Edition 45.

explain_wrong.B: Dispensing is not restricted to hospital settings. Community pharmacies can dispense duty-paid medicinal spirits on private prescription.
explain_wrong.C: Mixing ethanol with a denaturant before dispensing would change the medicinal product into denatured alcohol; this is not the correct action.
explain_wrong.D: Pure ethanol can be sold to individuals on a valid private prescription; refusal is not appropriate.
explain_wrong.E: FP10PCD forms are for Schedule 2 and 3 CD private prescriptions, not for medicinal alcohol. A standard private prescription suffices.

stem:
Lithium patient with rising creatinine/falling eGFR.

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Check for recent use of over-the-counter ibuprofen

options.label: B
options.text: Contact the prescriber regarding potential renal impairment

options.label: C
options.text: Dispense the medication as the lithium level is normal

options.label: D
options.text: Instruct the patient to increase their daily fluid intake to 3L

options.label: E
options.text: Suggest the prescriber stop lithium and start valproate immediately

explain_correct: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index and is renally cleared. Rising creatinine and falling eGFR suggest declining renal function, which increases lithium toxicity risk even if the current lithium level is in range. The pharmacist must contact the prescriber promptly so dose adjustment, monitoring intensification, or alternative therapy can be considered. Lithium also causes long-term renal toxicity, so deteriorating function may itself be drug-related.
citation: BNF lithium monograph. NICE CG185 (Bipolar disorder). MHRA Drug Safety Update on lithium toxicity.

explain_wrong.A: NSAID use is relevant (NSAIDs raise lithium levels by reducing renal clearance), but checking is one diagnostic step — the priority action is contacting the prescriber, not informally screening OTC use.
explain_wrong.C: Dispensing on a 'normal' lithium level ignores the renal trend. Lithium levels can be normal when renal function is failing because the patient is concentrating the drug despite reduced clearance.
explain_wrong.D: Increasing fluid to 3L is not a substitute for clinical review. Aggressive fluid loading without clinical assessment can be harmful.
explain_wrong.E: Suggesting the prescriber switch to valproate is outside the pharmacist's scope without clinical context. Valproate has its own teratogenicity / hepatotoxicity considerations and is not a like-for-like substitute.

stem:
Warfarin patient prescribed clarithromycin.

Which is the most appropriate advice regarding the interaction between these medications?

options.label: A
options.text: Double the warfarin dose for the duration of the antibiotic course

options.label: B
options.text: Ensure INR monitoring is performed within 3 days of starting the antibiotic

options.label: C
options.text: No change to monitoring is required for a short 7-day course

options.label: D
options.text: Stop taking warfarin until the antibiotic course is finished

options.label: E
options.text: Switch from warfarin to a DOAC to avoid the interaction

explain_correct: Clarithromycin is a CYP3A4 inhibitor and inhibits warfarin metabolism, leading to a rise in INR and increased bleeding risk. The standard recommendation is to check INR within 3 days of starting clarithromycin (or another macrolide such as erythromycin) so the warfarin dose can be adjusted promptly.
citation: BNF warfarin and clarithromycin interactions. SmPC clarithromycin. NICE CKS Anticoagulation - oral.

explain_wrong.A: Doubling the warfarin dose without monitoring is unsafe; the interaction raises INR, so increasing warfarin would compound bleeding risk.
explain_wrong.C: A 7-day course is long enough to cause a significant INR rise. Monitoring is required regardless of duration.
explain_wrong.D: Stopping warfarin entirely puts the patient at thrombotic risk. The interaction is managed by INR monitoring and dose adjustment, not cessation.
explain_wrong.E: Switching to a DOAC mid-course of antibiotics is not a routine response. Some DOACs also interact with clarithromycin (e.g. via P-gp / CYP3A4).

stem:
Humalog (rapid) prescribed for bedtime instead of Lantus (basal).

Which is the most appropriate action for the pharmacist to take?

options.label: A
options.text: Contact the prescriber to change the prescription to a basal insulin

options.label: B
options.text: Dispense the medication as rapid-acting insulin is commonly used at night

options.label: C
options.text: Dispense the medication with a supplied glucagon emergency kit

options.label: D
options.text: Halve the dose to minimize the risk of nocturnal hypoglycaemia

options.label: E
options.text: Instruct the patient to take the dose with a large carbohydrate-rich snack

explain_correct: Humalog (insulin lispro) is a rapid-acting insulin used at mealtimes. Lantus (insulin glargine) is a long-acting basal insulin used once daily. Prescribing rapid-acting insulin at bedtime without food would cause severe nocturnal hypoglycaemia. The pharmacist must contact the prescriber to clarify and change to the intended basal insulin.
citation: BNF insulin monographs. NICE NG28 (Type 2 diabetes). NPSA Patient Safety Alert on insulin.

explain_wrong.B: Rapid-acting insulin is NOT used at bedtime in standard regimens. This would cause hypoglycaemia.
explain_wrong.C: Supplying glucagon does not address the underlying prescribing error; the wrong insulin would still be used.
explain_wrong.D: Halving the dose still risks hypoglycaemia and does not correct the basal/bolus error.
explain_wrong.E: Taking with a carbohydrate snack does not match the duration of rapid-acting insulin to overnight glucose needs; basal insulin is required for overnight cover.

stem:
Child poor coordination with pMDI.

Which is the most appropriate intervention to improve medication delivery?

options.label: A
options.text: Administer the dose only while the child is asleep

options.label: B
options.text: Double the prescribed dose to compensate for poor technique

options.label: C
options.text: Provide a large-volume spacer device to use with the pMDI

options.label: D
options.text: Teach the child the 'open-mouth' inhalation technique

options.label: E
